I.A.1.text: Compare the scope, purpose, and relationship of WCAG 2.0, WCAG 2.1, WCAG 2.2, ATAG 2.0, and EN 301 549, and identify how each influences web accessibility design and conformance.

I.A.2.text: Explain the structure of WCAG 2.2, including the POUR principles, guidelines, success criteria, and conformance levels A, AA, and AAA.

I.A.3.text: Differentiate between normative and non-normative materials in WCAG and explain why that distinction matters for conformance.

I.A.4.text: Apply W3C techniques, including sufficient techniques, failures, and advisory techniques, to specific WCAG success criteria in implementation scenarios.

I.A.5.text: Explain the rationale for using current WCAG versions and summarize the accessibility impact of changes introduced in WCAG 2.1 and 2.2.

I.A.6.text: Differentiate between normative and non-normative materials in WAI-ARIA and explain why that distinction matters for conformance.

I.A.7.text: Explain the purpose of WAI-ARIA 1.2 and describe its model of roles, states, and properties.

I.A.8.text: Determine when WAI-ARIA should be used instead of native HTML and apply the five rules for using ARIA correctly.

I.A.9.text: Differentiate between normative and non-normative materials in ATAG 2.0 and explain why that distinction matters for conformance.

I.A.10.text: Explain how ATAG 2.0 applies to web content authoring tools and describe the intent of its two main parts.

I.A.11.text: Distinguish between accessibility features that authoring tools can automate and those requiring author input, and evaluate the benefits and limitations of automated authoring support.

I.A.12.text: Identify the technologies addressed by EN 301 549 and compare its relationship to WCAG.

I.A.13.text: Identify EN 301 549 requirements beyond WCAG, including Annex A and Web Accessibility Directive implications.

I.A.14.text: Interpret the self-scoping applicability language used in EN 301 549.

I.B.1.text: Demonstrate how to make JavaScript event handling device-independent for keyboard, pointer, and other input methods.

I.B.2.text: Explain progressive enhancement and the accessibility implications of unavailable or disabled JavaScript.

I.B.3.text: Identify accessibility risks created by dynamic content updates and determine appropriate strategies for focus management and user notification.

I.B.4.text: Distinguish between semantic HTML controls and non-semantic elements in terms of built-in accessibility and keyboard support.

I.B.5.text: Analyze how JavaScript implementations, event handling patterns, focus order, and ARIA state changes can improve or impair accessibility and usability for assistive technology users.

I.C.1.text: Construct custom interactive widgets using WAI-ARIA authoring practices, including correct roles, states, properties, and semantic structure.

I.C.2.text: Demonstrate and apply established keyboard interaction models for ARIA-based custom widgets during design and implementation.

I.C.3.text: Apply established keyboard interaction conventions when testing ARIA-based custom widgets.

I.C.4.text: Analyze interactive components and ARIA-based widgets for expected keyboard and screen reader behavior.

I.C.5.text: Analyze interactive components to determine whether gesture-based interactions provide single-pointer alternatives.

I.C.6.text: Evaluate custom controls and widget implementations to determine whether they follow accessibility authoring practices and contain correct ARIA markup.

I.C.7.text: Evaluate interactive components based on keyboard navigation and screen reader behavior.

I.C.8.text: Evaluate when native HTML controls should be used instead of custom ARIA widgets based on accessibility best practices and implementation complexity.

I.D.1.text: Explain the five primary rules of ARIA usage and their importance in accessible interface design.

I.D.2.text: Implement accessible names and descriptions for interactive controls using appropriate naming and description techniques.

I.D.3.text: Demonstrate correct focus management in interactive widgets using techniques such as roving tabindex and aria-activedescendant.

I.D.4.text: Explain how WAI-ARIA communicates semantics to assistive technologies, including roles, states, properties, and the accessibility tree.

I.D.5.text: Describe the semantic structure of ARIA roles, including required parent-child relationships and associated attributes.

I.D.6.text: Identify appropriate ARIA roles for common interactive widgets and structural page elements.

I.D.7.text: Analyze interface patterns and implementation scenarios to determine when native HTML or WAI-ARIA is the appropriate solution.

I.D.8.text: Analyze widget implementations to determine whether ARIA roles, states, and properties are applied correctly.

I.D.9.text: Analyze how ARIA roles, states, and properties can communicate dynamic interface changes in complex web applications.

I.D.10.text: Apply ARIA live regions to communicate dynamic content updates without moving keyboard focus when appropriate.

I.D.11.text: Apply ARIA properties and states to communicate widget state and dynamic changes in user interfaces.

I.D.12.text: Compare the accessibility benefits and limitations of native HTML controls and custom WAI-ARIA widgets.

I.D.13.text: Evaluate techniques for notifying assistive technologies about dynamic content changes, including the use of ARIA live regions.

I.D.14.text: Evaluate scenarios where the application role may be used and justify when it should be avoided.

I.D.15.text: Explain how applying an ARIA role overrides the native semantic role of an HTML element and the resulting accessibility implications.

I.E.1.text: Identify how assistive technologies interact with browsers and other user agents, and explain what accessibility support means under WCAG.

I.E.2.text: Assess the compatibility of technology choices with major screen reader-browser combinations and recommend fallbacks, workarounds, or polyfills where appropriate.

I.E.3.text: Explain how touch interaction changes when screen readers are enabled and demonstrate design patterns that support those interactions.

I.E.4.text: Analyze whether accessibility problems arise from design or implementation flaws versus limitations in browser or assistive technology support.

I.E.5.text: Distinguish between accessibility support that can be implemented automatically by authoring tools and support that requires author input, and evaluate the capabilities and limitations of automation.

I.E.6.text: Explain how ARIA can enhance assistive technology support for dynamic content and custom user interface components.

I.F.1.text: Compare the accessibility benefits and tradeoffs of native HTML controls and custom ARIA-based controls.

I.F.2.text: Demonstrate how to convert a non-semantic element into an accessible custom control using proper semantics, keyboard interaction, and visible focus.

I.F.3.text: Recommend the use of standard HTML controls whenever possible and justify the business and user benefits of doing so.

I.G.1.text: Demonstrate how to announce dynamic SPA page updates when navigation or content changes do not trigger a full page reload.

I.G.2.text: Implement focus changes appropriately when new SPA views or dialogs appear.

I.G.3.text: Analyze dynamic SPA content updates to determine when users should be notified of newly loaded content.

I.G.4.text: Explain accessibility challenges associated with single-page applications, including focus management, dynamic content updates, and assistive technology compatibility.

I.G.5.text: Evaluate the accessibility of a single-page application with assistive technologies and propose solutions for discovered gaps.

I.G.6.text: Evaluate whether focus indicators, labels, instructions, and touch targets remain usable under zoom, text-spacing, and touch interaction conditions.

I.H.1.text: Describe strategies that people with blindness, low vision, motor disabilities, auditory disabilities, and cognitive or learning disabilities use to interact with web content.

I.H.2.text: Analyze how design elements such as headings, landmarks, and color contrast affect navigation and comprehension for diverse user groups.

I.H.3.text: Propose structural and layout revisions that better support multiple disability groups.

II.A.1.text: Identify accessibility issues that arise from specific browser and screen-reader combinations.

II.A.2.text: Explain why certain HTML or ARIA features may have support gaps across browsers or assistive technologies and how to mitigate those gaps.

II.A.3.text: Evaluate accessibility across devices, platforms, browsers, and assistive technologies.

II.A.4.text: Analyze how assistive technologies, adaptive strategies, and user agents interact to shape accessibility support.

II.B.1.text: Map accessibility barriers to the appropriate WCAG success criterion or EN 301 549 requirement and distinguish conformance failures from broader usability or best-practice issues.

II.B.2.text: Apply the WCAG-EM methodology to define scope, select a representative sample, evaluate content, and report findings.

II.B.3.text: Explain WCAG conformance levels and analyze factors that influence the selection of a target conformance level.

II.B.4.text: Explain WCAG conformance requirements and how they apply to evaluating web content.

II.B.5.text: Describe limitations in WCAG and propose best-practice enhancements for issues not fully covered by the specifications.

II.B.6.text: Analyze examples of web content to determine whether they meet or fail specific WCAG success criteria.

II.B.7.text: Identify WCAG 2.2 success criteria and classify them by conformance level.

II.B.8.text: Differentiate between sufficient techniques, advisory techniques, and failures associated with WCAG success criteria.

II.B.9.text: Distinguish between normative and non-normative information within accessibility specifications.

II.B.10.text: Identify relevant accessibility requirements in WCAG 2.2, WAI-ARIA, and ATAG when evaluating accessibility issues.

II.B.11.text: Explain the relationship between WCAG principles, guidelines, and success criteria and the accessibility impact of each principle.

II.B.12.text: Compare WCAG with EN 301 549, including how WCAG requirements are incorporated and which additional EN 301 549 requirements extend beyond WCAG.

II.B.13.text: Analyze the scope and intent of EN 301 549 requirements in Annex A Table A.1 that go beyond WCAG.

II.B.14.text: Identify the technologies and digital products addressed by EN 301 549.

II.B.15.text: Explain how ATAG 2.0 applies to authoring tools and development environments, including the purpose and scope of its two primary sections.

II.B.16.text: Differentiate between accessibility issues that can be detected by automated tools and those that require manual evaluation.

II.B.17.text: Differentiate between accessibility and user experience design, including their goals, assumptions, and areas of overlap.

II.B.18.text: Analyze how assistive technology users may interact differently with web content based on skill level, navigation strategies, and personal preferences.

II.B.19.text: Analyze dynamic content updates to determine when keyboard focus should be moved to newly loaded content.

II.B.20.text: Explain the major additions introduced in WCAG 2.1, particularly those addressing new input methods and improved support for low vision and cognitive disabilities.

II.B.21.text: Apply standard keyboard interaction patterns to test native HTML interactive elements.

II.C.1.text: Conduct screen reader testing to verify that interactive elements expose correct name, role, value, state, and status changes.

II.C.2.text: Demonstrate manual keyboard testing to detect missing focus indicators, incorrect tab order, keyboard traps, and other keyboard-operability issues.

II.C.3.text: Use magnifier, high-contrast, zoom, and viewport testing to detect low-vision accessibility problems.

II.C.4.text: Evaluate whether captioning, transcripts, and other alternatives meet common user needs for auditory disabilities.

II.C.5.text: Evaluate HTML markup to determine whether headings, lists, tables, landmarks, and other structural semantics correctly convey meaning to assistive technologies.

II.C.6.text: Evaluate navigation structures, headings, labels, and link descriptions to determine whether they support clear information architecture and efficient screen reader navigation.

II.C.7.text: Evaluate form implementations to verify accessible interaction patterns, programmatic error identification, clear instructions, and usable suggestions for correction.

II.C.8.text: Test the reading order of web content to confirm that the programmatic order is meaningful and consistent with intended visual presentation.

II.C.9.text: Evaluate whether text alternatives are provided appropriately for images, video, and other non-text content.

II.C.10.text: Evaluate accessibility solutions to ensure they follow web standards rather than relying on technology-specific quirks, and judge when supplementing browser or assistive technology behavior may be appropriate.

II.C.11.text: Evaluate the limitations of guidelines, principles, and techniques when assessing real-world accessibility issues.

II.C.12.text: Verify that page language, form errors, and component states are programmatically conveyed to assistive technologies.

II.D.1.text: Compare the strengths and limitations of automated accessibility testing tools, browser-based tools, spider tools, and bookmarklets.

II.D.2.text: Use site-wide scanning tools and browser-based developer tools to detect markup, ARIA, and structural accessibility issues.

II.D.3.text: Interpret automated test results by confirming valid failures, identifying false positives and omissions, and determining appropriate manual follow-up.

II.D.4.text: Analyze how accessibility testing tools can be applied and integrated at different stages of the development lifecycle.

II.D.5.text: Explain the purpose of ACT Rules and their role in standardizing automated accessibility testing.

II.D.6.text: Identify common categories of accessibility testing tools.

II.D.7.text: Inspect HTML structure and accessibility-related attributes using browser developer tools.

II.D.8.text: Demonstrate use of color-contrast analyzers, focus inspectors, or validators to confirm partial WCAG compliance.

II.E.1.text: Explain how accessibility quality assurance should be integrated into agile and waterfall lifecycles.

II.E.2.text: Establish an ongoing accessibility testing and regression process that combines automated and manual methods.

II.E.3.text: Coordinate with product owners, designers, developers, and content creators to include accessibility in acceptance criteria, definitions of done, and broader lifecycle responsibilities.

II.E.4.text: Explain how accessibility maturity models and assessment tools can be used to measure progress and support sustainability.

II.F.1.text: Demonstrate use of popular screen reader and browser combinations to confirm accessibility across environments.

II.F.2.text: Verify that dynamically updated or hidden elements are correctly announced to, or hidden from, screen readers.

II.F.3.text: Assess whether alternative input methods such as switch control or voice input can navigate and operate interactive elements effectively.

II.F.4.text: Evaluate whether interactive elements use native controls or appropriate ARIA semantics to support keyboard, voice control, and alternative input devices.

II.G.1.text: Perform low-vision usability walk-throughs using zoom, high-contrast, and related settings to identify layout and text-flow issues.

II.G.2.text: Assess mobile and touch interaction to ensure content remains accessible on smaller screens and does not require two-dimensional scrolling.

II.G.3.text: Identify cognitive barriers in web content and recommend simpler language, clearer instructions, or multi-step guidance.

II.G.4.text: Evaluate whether motion-based, device-motion, and gesture-based interactions provide accessible alternatives or can be disabled.

II.G.5.text: Evaluate multimedia to verify that videos provide captions and audio-only media provide transcripts.

II.G.6.text: Evaluate the effects of contrast modes, text and non-text contrast, and color-contrast requirements on accessibility.

II.G.7.text: Evaluate time limits, moving content, and automatically playing media to determine whether users can control or disable them.

II.G.8.text: Explain how touch interactions change when screen readers are enabled on mobile devices.

II.G.9.text: Identify accessibility issues related to sensory-dependent instructions, autoplaying audio, or moving content that may interfere with assistive technology use.

II.G.10.text: Test device orientation support to ensure content and functionality are available in both portrait and landscape orientations.

II.G.11.text: Verify that text can be resized up to 200% without loss of functionality or content.

III.A.1.text: Categorize accessibility issues by severity and formulate a remediation sequence that accounts for user impact, legal risk, effort, and resource constraints.

III.A.2.text: Justify which accessibility issues should be addressed first based on legal risk, user impact, and overall usability.

III.A.3.text: Communicate high-impact accessibility barriers clearly to stakeholders and developers.

III.A.4.text: Evaluate the importance and urgency of dynamically loaded content to determine the most appropriate notification strategy for assistive technology users.

III.B.1.text: Propose accessible and feasible remediation strategies for conformance failures using native HTML or ARIA-based solutions.

III.B.2.text: Differentiate between targeted fixes and broader redesign approaches when addressing accessibility barriers.

III.B.3.text: Collaborate with design and development teams to implement shared UI patterns or code solutions that prevent repeated accessibility faults.

III.B.4.text: Analyze automated and manual testing results to determine appropriate remediation or follow-up testing steps.

III.B.5.text: Assess whether an ideal solution or a good-enough solution is appropriate given project constraints, target users, and available resources.

III.B.6.text: Communicate the purpose, approach, and expected outcomes of remediation strategies to relevant stakeholders.

III.B.7.text: Evaluate remediation options to determine the most practical and broadly applicable solution for a given accessibility issue.

III.B.8.text: Evaluate the benefits of integrating accessibility during design and development compared with addressing accessibility after development.

III.B.9.text: Identify stakeholders needed to plan and implement accessibility remediation.

III.B.10.text: Incorporate feedback from users with disabilities to validate and refine remediation strategies.

III.C.1.text: Evaluate vendor Accessibility Conformance Reports and differentiate among ACR types to determine product compatibility with organizational accessibility standards.

III.C.2.text: Recommend contract language and procurement checklist requirements that obligate vendors to address accessibility defects and meet relevant standards.

III.C.3.text: Implement a vendor management process that includes accessibility roadmap review, defect remediation, monitoring, and mitigation planning.

III.C.4.text: Analyze compatibility and interoperability issues across combinations of browsers, platforms, operating systems, devices, and assistive technologies.

III.C.5.text: Assess the feasibility of proposed remediation solutions across different project contexts and technical environments.

III.C.6.text: Describe how device-independent event handling supports accessibility across keyboard, mouse, touch, and assistive technology interactions.

III.C.7.text: Evaluate accessibility claims, conformance statements, and design decisions in procurement documentation.

III.C.8.text: Explain how accessibility requirements can be incorporated into organizational procurement processes and the role of accessibility experts in vendor evaluation.

III.C.9.text: Identify accessibility risks raised during procurement evaluations.
